steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Standard English Conventions question testing the serial-semicolon rule. The sentence lists three deserts, and each list item contains an internal comma (e.g., “the Sonoran Desert, ranked fifteenth in size”). When list items contain commas, semicolons must separate the items to prevent misreading.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — fifteenth in size, the Great Salt Desert; ranked thirty-first,: the semicolon falls between “the Great Salt Desert” and “ranked thirty-first,” splitting the desert’s name from its rank. This is a misplaced semicolon that severs a noun from its modifier. ✗
  • Choice B — fifteenth in size; the Great Salt Desert, ranked thirty-first;: semicolons fall after the first item (“Sonoran Desert, ranked fifteenth in size”) and after the second item (“the Great Salt Desert, ranked thirty-first”), correctly separating all three items while keeping each desert’s rank attached to its name. ✓
  • Choice C — fifteenth in size the Great Salt Desert, ranked thirty-first: no semicolons at all. Without semicolons, the list-item boundaries are unclear and the first two desert entries run together. ✗
  • Choice D — fifteenth in size; the Great Salt Desert; ranked thirty-first;: the second semicolon falls between “the Great Salt Desert” and “ranked thirty-first,” again separating the desert from its rank and creating a fragment (“ranked thirty-first”) as a standalone item. ✗
That is the correct answer.
